title: The X-Ray
synopsis: The pandemic did not break anything. This is the thing I keep coming back to, the observation that I think gets lost in the urgency of the crisis r...
preview: The pandemic did not break anything. This is the thing I keep coming back to, the observation that I think gets lost in the urgency of the crisis r...
content:
The pandemic did not break anything. This is the thing I keep coming back to, the observation that I think gets lost in the urgency of the crisis response. COVID-19 did not break the healthcare system or the supply chains or the manufacturing base or the strategic reserves. It revealed that they were already broken. The virus is an X-ray. It shows the skeleton. And the skeleton, it turns out, has fractures we have been ignoring for a long time.

Start with the masks.

Australia cannot make surgical masks in sufficient quantity to supply its own healthcare workers during a pandemic. This is not a novel discovery. The vulnerability was documented in reports, flagged in reviews, mentioned in the kind of bureaucratic assessments that are produced, acknowledged, filed, and forgotten. We knew we could not make masks. We did not make the capacity to make masks. When the masks were needed, they were not there.

This is a microcosm. Scale it up.

We cannot make ventilators. We import the majority of our medical devices. We import roughly ninety percent of our pharmaceuticals. The supply chains for critical medications run through factories in India and China, and when those factories slowed or stopped in the early weeks of the pandemic, the medications became scarce. Not unavailable, not yet, but scarce in the way that makes pharmacists anxious and health administrators do contingency arithmetic on the backs of envelopes.

The healthcare system itself was already running at capacity before the pandemic arrived. This is the dirty secret of efficient healthcare management: efficiency means no slack. No empty beds. No idle staff. No surplus equipment. The system is optimised for normal demand, and normal demand is all it can handle. When demand spikes, as it does during a pandemic, there is nothing in reserve. The surge capacity does not exist because surge capacity is expensive and the budget does not fund expensive things that sit unused most of the time.

The supply chains tell the same story. Just-in-time delivery, the logistical philosophy that has been adopted by virtually every industry, means that warehouses are small and inventories are minimal. Products arrive as they are needed, not before. This is efficient. It reduces storage costs, minimises waste, optimises capital allocation. It also means that any disruption, a factory closure, a shipping delay, a border restriction, immediately translates into a shortage. There is no buffer. The system has been optimised so thoroughly that the concept of buffer has been designed out of it.

The toilet paper shortage was absurd, but it was also instructive. People saw empty shelves and panicked, which emptied more shelves, which created more panic. The supply of toilet paper had not actually declined. The demand had spiked, and the supply chain had no capacity to absorb the spike because it was not designed to absorb spikes. It was designed for steady-state conditions, and steady-state conditions are what we had until we did not.

The digital infrastructure dependency became visible overnight. When offices closed and work moved online, the entire economy became dependent on video conferencing, cloud computing, and collaboration platforms. All American. All operated by companies headquartered in jurisdictions that are not ours, subject to laws that are not ours, optimised for priorities that are not ours.

Zoom is an American company. Microsoft Teams is an American product. The cloud servers are in American data centres, operated under American law. When Australian businesses, schools, and government agencies moved their operations onto these platforms, they moved their operations onto foreign infrastructure. This was not discussed. It was not debated. It was done, because there was no alternative, because we had not built an alternative, because building alternatives is expensive and inefficient and the market had provided perfectly good products from elsewhere.

Every one of these fragilities was known before the pandemic. Every one had been documented. Reports existed. Warnings had been issued. The healthcare system's lack of surge capacity had been identified. The supply chain vulnerabilities had been mapped. The manufacturing hollowing had been observed and lamented.

Nobody acted. Not because the information was hidden but because acting on it would have required spending money on resilience rather than efficiency, and the political system rewards efficiency. Efficiency is visible in budgets, in quarterly reports, in the metrics that administrators and politicians use to demonstrate competence. Resilience is invisible. It sits in warehouses, in unused capacity, in capabilities that are maintained but rarely deployed. You cannot point to resilience on a spreadsheet. You can only point to its absence, and by then it is too late.

The pandemic has exposed the skeleton. The fractures are visible. The question is what we do with the image.

The temptation, and I think it will prove overwhelming, is to treat this as a crisis requiring a crisis response. Emergency funding. Temporary measures. A period of heightened attention followed by a return to normal. The virus will be defeated, or managed, or accepted, and the urgency will fade, and the structural vulnerabilities that the virus exposed will remain, because addressing them requires the kind of sustained, expensive, politically unrewarding investment that our system is not designed to produce.

We will return to normal. Normal is the problem.
